findings.description: Dash style difference: PDF uses em dashes (–) while HTML uses en dashes (–) or plain hyphens in some places
findings.evidence: PDF uses '–' consistently (e.g., 'Field Marking – Field Markings'), HTML uses '–' which appears as en dashes in rendering

findings.description: The HTML version has slightly different margins and spacing, with content stretching wider across the page compared to the PDF's more constrained text width
findings.evidence: PDF has wider left/right margins with text in a narrower column; HTML text spans more of the page width

findings.description: The superscript formatting on ordinal indicators (e.g., '16th', '12th') appears slightly different between PDF and HTML
findings.evidence: PDF shows '16th' and '12th' with superscript 'th'; HTML also shows superscript but rendering may differ slightly

findings.description: No heading elements used in the HTML. The document has no formal title or section headings in the PDF either, so this is understandable, but the 'Ongoing Activities' text could have been rendered as a heading for better structure.
findings.evidence:
PDF starts with 'Ongoing Activities:' as a label. HTML renders it as a plain

tag with no heading hierarchy.

findings.description: The HTML contains nested duplicate <main> elements, which constitutes structural duplication of the content wrapper.
findings.evidence:
HTML has
inside , then another
nested inside: '
\n
\n

Ongoing Activities:

...'
